sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit: The Forge Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member offboarding & exit interviews** – no documented process for departures, churn analysis, or win-back triggers
- **Complaint & incident logging** – no standardised pathway for member complaints, coach conduct issues, or safety incidents (GDPR/liability risk)
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no SOP for P&L review, class revenue tracking, or payment disputes
- **Staffing handover & absence cover** – no protocol for coaching shift swaps, sick cover, or cross-training sign-off
- **Member health screening & medical flags** – no intake process for injuries, medications, or pregnancy declarations
- **Retention analytics & intervention** – no trigger-based process for at-risk members (attendance drop, non-renewals)
- **Guest passes & trial member journey** – no standardised conversion or data capture SOP
- **Complaints resolution & escalation** – distinct from logging; no tiered response protocol

**Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding** – likely missing: probation milestones, peer observation checklist, member feedback loops, certification renewal tracking
- **Class scheduling** – no seasonal demand planning or capacity stress-testing process
- **Member communication cadence** – no SOP for milestone emails, abandoned-session follow-ups, or lapsed-member re-engagement campaigns
- **Health & safety incident response** – if exists, probably lacks: evacuation drill frequency, equipment failure protocol, or incident timeline documentation
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – gap likely: paid ad performance review cycle, or campaign post-mortem process
- **New program launch** – no pre-launch comms, coach briefing, or member feedback collection SOP

**Build next**

1. **Member Exit & Churn Recovery** – 2-part S

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REVIEW & ACTION PLAN

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing review: AR/M collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June).** You've gained 23 net members since relocation but revenue per member is down 29%. Audit your membership tier mix — are new joiners on discounted plans? Run a cohort analysis of Dec–April joiners vs May–June cohort. Increase new member price by 10–15% from July; grandfather existing members for 3 months, then migrate to standard rate.

• **Churn spike in April–May (8.33% and 5.74%) post-relocation is a red flag.** June improved to 0%, which is excellent — but you've lost 41 members gross since April peak (108→147 net masks turnover). Dedicate one staff member to win-back calls and referral incentives (£10–15 credit) targeting lapsed members. Target: hold attrition below 2%.

• **Upsell PT and class packs.** June revenue of £17,711 on 147 members suggests most are on base membership. Cross-sell 1-to-1 sessions at £45–55/hour and 10-class top-ups at £99. Even 5 PT sessions/month from 20% of the member base = £4,400+ incremental revenue.

• **Quiet class utilisation.** Identify 2–3 lowest-attendance time slots (typically early morning or midweek afternoon). Either consolidate and reallocate the instructor, or run a 4-week "bring a friend free" promotion to fill them and convert guests.

---

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people costs are the biggest issue.** May £9,233 → June £9,390 is stable, but Jan–Apr show extreme volatility (peaking £30,511 in April relocation month). Remove April from analysis: Jan–Mar averaged £11,573; May–June average £9,312. **You've improved 20% post-relocation.** Identify what drove Jan–Mar spike (utilities setup, equipment, cleaning contracts?) and ensure June baseline of £9,390 is the new run-rate. If utilities/rent are higher in the new studio, this is acceptable to support growth — but audit every line item against the previous studio's costs to confirm no waste.

• **Payroll (people) at £3,686 in June on £17,711 revenue = 20.8%.** This is healthy and aligned with fitness industry targets (18–22%). However

staff.advice:
• **Move Grace's Build: Lower (17 sessions) to JoJo.** JoJo is the strongest coach and already leads Build: Upper; consolidating lower-body strength coaching with one expert prevents quality drift and gives members consistency. This is JoJo's core strength.

• **Shift Grace's Elevate (6 sessions) and Strongevity (3 sessions) to Eilis.** Eilis has already coached Elevate successfully; these lower-intensity classes suit her current development level and build her confidence without Tuesday complexity.

• **Redistribute Grace's No Sweat Intro (3 sessions) across both coaches.** These are low-technical onboarding sessions—Eilis takes 2, JoJo takes 1. Spreads the load fairly and gives Eilis valuable member-facing time.

• **Stop counting Body Composition scans as coaching load.** Grace's 9 scans and JoJo's 20 are administrative, not delivery. Hand these to any available admin or rotating coach assistant time to free up real coaching capacity.

• **JoJo is now carrying ~120 real coaching sessions (PT + classes).** This is high but sustainable given her Head Coach role. Watch her PT load—if it exceeds 50 sessions/month, move 5–8 lower-priority PT slots to Eilis to develop her 1:1 skills.

• **Eilis sits at ~40 real sessions and has capacity.** By June, she should absorb 12–15 of Grace's hours, landing her at 52–55 sessions—a manageable development load that prevents either coach burning out.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. Add a "Open Gym + Coach" slot on Monday or Thursday evenings — members pay a small fee to access the studio with a coach available for form checks or solo workouts, capturing time that's currently empty between scheduled classes.

2. Run a monthly "Mobility + Strength Workshop" (60 mins) on Saturday mornings before your quietest period — position it as a paid add-on focusing on injury prevention or pre-lift prep, drawing from your Build class regulars who may skip weekends.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. Move **Engine** and **Capacity** to Tuesday or Wednesday (your busiest days) at off-peak times like 6:30am or 7pm to piggyback on higher gym traffic, then run a 2-week "free trial" for members who've done 10+ Build classes — these are conditioning complements to strength work.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — The Forge

**1. Start with your ACTUAL delivery reality, not aspirational pricing**
Before writing anything down, have 30-min interviews with 2-3 of your coaches: "What do you actually deliver in a session? How long? What's realistic week-to-week?" Document what's *happening now*, then price it. Generic menus kill credibility fast.

**2. Split the coach menu into "what we sell" vs "how you deliver it"**
Coaches need: session formats (1-on-1, small group, drop-in), duration options, any prep/follow-up they own, and clear boundaries (e.g., "nutrition advice stops here, refer to X"). This prevents scope creep and confusion on what they're accountable for.

**3. Make the member menu a *decision tree*, not a list**
Don't just list prices. Structure it like: "New to strength? → Pick Foundations 6-week block. Want ongoing? → Choose membership tier (unlimited/4x/2x per month) + add nutrition coaching as addon." Members make faster decisions when the menu answers their actual question.

**4. Tag pricing with ONE differentiator per service**
"Small group capped at 6" or "Coach gives weekly form video" — specificity justifies your price and gives coaches talking points. Generic "personal training" doesn't compete; "Form-focused 1-on-1 for women over 40" does.

**5. Lock it in a shared doc coaches can reference during trial/consultation**
Google Sheet, Notion, or Canva — doesn't matter. What matters: coaches can pull it up in a browser during a potential member conversation.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement - Execution Plan for Grace

• **Audit your actual drop-off points first** — Spend 1 hour reviewing Google Analytics to see WHERE people leave (homepage? pricing? class details?). That's where your money is leaking. Fix the biggest leak before redesigning everything.

• **Mine your own FAQ from real conversations** — Pull the last 20 enquiry emails, WhatsApp chats, and trial signup questions. The exact language women use to ask = the exact language you should use to answer. Don't guess what women want clarified.

• **Create one "anti-confusion" section per page** — Women's-only gyms trigger specific questions: safety/privacy, beginner-friendly?, childcare, changing facilities, judgement-free vibe. Pick the 3 biggest objections *for your audience* and answer each in one visible sentence + expandable detail. Test which resonates.

• **A/B test your headline on your existing traffic this week** — Don't wait for a full redesign. Change your homepage headline to something like "No Men. No Mirrors. No Ego." for 50% of visitors, measure clicks-to-trial against your current version. Fastest way to validate what actually converts.

• **Have 3 current members review the pages** — They know your real value prop. Ask: "Would this have sold YOU?" Their feedback is worth more than any design template.

What's your biggest current objection you hear repeatedly from prospects?

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Implement Member Feedback System – The Forge

**Start stupidly small, then scale:**
- Launch with ONE question on a 2-minute post-class form: "What would make you come back more often?" Google Forms link via QR code at exit. Collect 30 responses before iterating. This beats designing a perfect system that never launches.

**Make it behaviorally sticky (not voluntary):**
- Don't rely on willpower. Hand physical feedback cards with post-class water bottles for 4 weeks. You'll get 5-10x more responses than a poster asking people to fill something out. Track which class times/instructors get highest engagement too.

**Turn data into visible action fast:**
- Pick the ONE most-requested thing (childcare hours? Saturday strength class? better changing room lighting?) and implement it within 4 weeks. Announce it in the gym with "This feedback from YOU made this happen." Repeats the feedback loop. Members feel heard, not surveyed.

**Extract business intel, not just complaints:**
- Track responses by membership tier and tenure. New members' feedback ≠ 2-year members' feedback. You'll spot retention risks early and upsell opportunities (e.g., if people want nutrition coaching, there's revenue).

**Route the data somewhere it gets *used*:**
- One person owns the monthly summary (assign this now—15 mins/month). Dump it in a Slack channel or email to your team by the 5th. If feedback dies in a spreadsheet, it's noise.

What's your biggest pain point right now—retention, attracting new members, or staff morale?

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution for The Forge

• **Start with revenue-impact order, not alphabetical:** Tackle personal training first (highest lifetime value + retention lever), then membership (largest cohort = biggest operational pain), then Fuel Forward. This forces you to nail your most profitable SOPs when energy is highest.

• **Use a 3-layer template, not 20-page manuals:** Member journey → staff checklist → troubleshooting. Example: *PT SOP Layer 1* = "First session to month-3 progression path," *Layer 2* = "How staff onboards PT client," *Layer 3* = "What to do if member misses 3 sessions." One-pagers beat binders.

• **Embed the "why" for each SOP with revenue attached:** Staff execute SOPs they understand make money. Write "SOP: Fuel Forward upsell at sign-up" with "→ 60% conversion = £X monthly incremental revenue" in the header. Changes compliance rates dramatically.

• **Record one video walkthrough per SOP, don't rewrite for different roles:** One PT onboarding video covers the process once—post it in your staff hub. Link the written SOP to it. Cuts creation time by 60% and ensures consistency.

• **Launch with a 2-week soft test on one service, then document exceptions:** Pick membership first, run it internally for 14 days, capture what actually happened vs. what you wrote. Revise once, then roll out Fuel Forward. Prevents the "beautiful SOP nobody follows" trap.

What's your biggest operational bottleneck right now—is it member drop-off, staff consistency, or onboarding speed

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar — Execution Playbook

• **Batch-create content THIS WEEK using your phone camera.** Pick one 90-minute session: film 8-12 short clips of members mid-workout, transformation moments, class highlights. Shoot in natural light, minimal editing needed. This gives you 2-3 weeks of raw footage instantly—no "finding time" later.

• **Use a free template (Canva or Buffer's content calendar)—don't build from scratch.** Populate it RIGHT NOW with your actual gym rhythms: Monday = motivation/Monday mindset post, Wednesday = mid-week class showcase, Friday = weekend challenge, Sunday = member spotlight. Lock this pattern so planning takes 30 mins/week, not hours of thinking.

• **Assign a 15-minute "content dump" every Sunday evening.** Grace schedules 3-4 posts for the following week in one sitting using Buffer or Meta's scheduling tool. No daily stress. This removes decision fatigue and guarantees consistency.

• **Mirror your class schedule to content pillars.** If you run strength Mondays and cardio Fridays, post mirrors that—repurposing what's happening anyway. Zero extra effort beyond filming what's already happening inside The Forge.

• **Track ONE metric that matters: engagement rate on posts (likes + comments ÷ followers).** After 4 weeks, double down on whatever content type wins. Kill posts getting <2% engagement. This ensures your effort compounds, not just fills a calendar.

**Start today: batch-film content + build your template. You're live by Friday.**

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing the Videography Schedule with Jordan

**Lock it in NOW, not "soon"**
- Block 8-week recurring calendar slots today (next session date + all future ones). Send Jordan a formal booking confirmation with deposit/retainer terms. Vague scheduling kills momentum—he'll pick up other clients.

**Create a content production brief, not a shoot list**
- Before each session, give Jordan 3-4 specific outcomes you need (e.g., "30-second member transformation story," "gym floor energy reel," "class highlight clip"). This beats showing up and hoping he captures useful stuff. Saves editing time too.

**Batch your content needs strategically**
- Don't shoot random clips every 8 weeks. Plan: who are you profiling (members with stories), what's seasonal (New Year reset, summer strength focus), what's underperforming on socials (testimonials, class promos). Give Jordan direction on what wins for you.

**Assign ONE person to liaise with Jordan**
- You or a manager owns the brief, scheduling, and direction—not scattered communication. Jordan needs clarity. This person also organizes member availability, location setup, and ensures you're actually using the content (most gyms shoot great footage and never post it).

**Measure what you're actually doing with it**
- Set a rule: every video goes live within 7 days + track engagement/signups from each piece. If they're not converting, you've wasted the 8-week cycle. Adjust what you're shooting based on what actually works.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing 6-Week Trial Enquiries — Concrete Action Plan

• **Audit your current leak points NOW** — Pull last month's trial enquiry data. Where do people drop off? (Email not sent? No follow-up? Wrong timing?) Fix THE bleeding point first, not everything. If 40% never get a response within 24hrs, automation won't save you—it's a staff responsiveness issue.

• **Build a 3-email sequence, not a complex workflow** — Day 0 (instant): welcome + gym access details + "start date confirmation needed." Day 3: friendly check-in + one success story from another member. Day 35: "Last week of trial—ready to join?" Make it manual-proof: same email, every time, zero thinking required.

• **Gate it behind one YES/NO decision** — When someone enquires, your staff answers ONE question in writing: "Did she confirm her start date?" If yes → sequence auto-triggers. If no → it goes to a "needs follow-up" list reviewed Mondays. Stop making this complicated.

• **Track the ONE metric that matters** — Trial-to-member conversion rate. You want to know: of people who started a trial, how many are still members 8 weeks later? Measure this monthly. If it's below 60%, automation won't fix a product/experience problem.

• **Set it live with ONE platform** — Zapier + your enquiry form + Gmail or Mailchimp. 30 mins to set up. Don't wait for "perfect"—launch week 1, tweak week 2.

What's your current trial-to-member conversion % right now?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist Setup — The Forge

**1. Start with their actual week, not a blank template**
Sit down with JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah separately for 15 mins each. Ask: "What 5-7 things do you actually do every week that move The Forge forward?" Document *their* reality first, then layer KPIs onto it. This beats pushing a generic checklist that'll gather dust.

**2. Assign one clear number per person that they can influence directly**
- **JoJo** (assume ops/classes): "New member sign-ups per week" + "Class attendance rate" — she can track both
- **Eilis** (assume sales/retention): "Member check-ins completed" + "Retention rate" — measurable weekly action
- **Sarah** (assume marketing/community): "Social posts published" + "Engagement rate" — she controls the input

Make sure they *own* the input metric (what they do), not just the output (what happens after).

**3. Build the checklist as a shared Google Sheet, not a document**
One row per task, columns for Mon-Fri, tick-off system. They update it daily. Grace reviews Friday at 3pm, takes 10 mins. This forces visibility without weekly meetings eating time.

**4. Set a "red flag threshold" for each KPI**
If JoJo's sign-ups drop below 3 per week, she flags it immediately — you don't wait for end-of-month reporting. This shifts the mindset from reporting to problem-solving in real time.

**What do their roles actually *are*?** If they're different,

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Execute This Vision Session Like a Pro

• **Pre-session homework (send 48hrs before):** Email the SSO and 90-day plan to your team with ONE question: "Which of these 3 areas will you own?" Forces them to self-select their lane instead of you assigning it. Reduces pushback and ownership questions in the room.

• **Structure the 60-min meeting as three 15-min blocks:** (1) SSO context + why it matters to *them personally* (commission impact? career growth?), (2) Walk the 90-day plan month-by-month with specific revenue/member targets they'll influence, (3) Each person states their lane + one metric they'll own. Record it. Send it back same day.

• **Make "lane" brutally specific:** Not "marketing" — it's "Instagram posts 3x weekly + member referral tracking." Not "operations" — it's "new-member onboarding flow + retention emails." Vague ownership kills execution.

• **End with a clarity checkpoint:** Ask each person: "If we hit our 90-day targets, what changes for you?" (Bonus hours? Bonus structure? New equipment?) This ties vision to their self-interest and surfaces what actually motivates them.

• **Publish a one-page visual** (Canva template, 5 mins) showing SSO → 90-day milestones → each person's face + lane. Post it in the gym. Reminds them daily this isn't just talk.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge (30 days)

**Week 1 — Get the data:**
- Have each team member (instructors, reception, management) log their actual hours + tasks for 7 days using a simple Google Form (3 fields: time block, task, difficulty level). Don't rely on memory or job descriptions — what they're *actually* doing matters.
- Pull membership data: who's teaching peak vs. off-peak classes, who's handling member complaints, who manages social media *and* admin. You'll see invisible workload clusters immediately.

**Week 2 — Spot the bottlenecks:**
- Map it visually: list each person + their current commitments. Look for anyone doing >35 hours + covering multiple skill areas (e.g., instructor + social media lead + onboarding). That's your over-stretched list.
- Ask directly in 1-on-1s: "What takes you longest?" and "What would free up 5 hours?" — people know; they're just waiting to be asked.

**Weeks 3-4 — Rebalance ruthlessly:**
- **Reassign, don't just add**: Move low-value tasks (admin paperwork, routine emails) *away* from your best instructors. Hire a part-time admin or shift it to reception if possible.
- **Compress peak load**: If someone's teaching 12+ classes weekly + managing bookings, cut them to 8 classes and hand bookings to someone else. Measure their effectiveness after, not before.
- **Create one quick win**: Automate something this month (class cancellation texts, membership renewal reminders) to

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Audit your top 3 cost categories THIS WEEK** — staff, utilities, and supplies likely account for 70%+ of spend. Pull last 3 months of P&Ls. Don't guess.

**Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW** — your gym equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, and utilities providers expect this annually. Get 2-3 competitive quotes, then use them as leverage. Target 8-12% cuts here; this alone could hit your 10%.

**Right-size staffing during low-traffic hours** — map your hourly member count for 2 weeks. If 6am-9am and 5pm-7pm are 70% of revenue, staffing mid-afternoon heavily is waste. Shift to part-time/on-call models for off-peak slots.

**Cut or consolidate software subscriptions** — most gyms stack 4-6 apps (booking, billing, marketing, comms). Audit what's actually driving member retention vs. what's just a habit. Cancel unused tools immediately.

**Bundle services to reduce marketing spend** — if you're spending heavily on individual campaigns, create package deals (3-month intro rate, refer-a-friend discounts). This cuts acquisition cost per member while increasing LTV.

**Which of these three resonates most for your gym's situation right now?** I can drill into the execution if you want specifics on one.

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs at The Forge

**1. Set a non-negotiable review rhythm**
Block 2 hours the first Friday of each month (same day, same time). Pull actuals vs. target for: member acquisition cost (CAC), lifetime value (LTV), churn rate, revenue per member, class utilization %. Do this *before* spending money on ads or new initiatives—numbers must drive decisions, not follow them.

**2. Track the metrics that actually move the needle for a gym**
Stop tracking vanity metrics. Focus on: (a) monthly recurring revenue (MRR) from memberships, (b) class attendance % (signals retention risk early), (c) personal training attach rate (high-margin revenue), (d) new member onboarding completion (predicts churn). These four tell you if The Forge is healthy or bleeding money.

**3. Create a one-page dashboard she updates herself**
Use a simple Google Sheet or Xero integration—don't outsource this. Grace needs to *see* the numbers weekly so patterns jump out. When CAC spikes or churn ticks up, she spots it in days, not weeks. This is where strategic decisions get made.

**4. Build a "variance narrative" habit**
Every month, write 3-4 sentences next to each KPI: "Why did this move?" Example: "Churn up 2% because summer holidays hit + need to text inactive members before they quit." This forces accountability and stops her guessing.

**5. Tie one KPI directly to next month's action**
If churn is the weak point, next month's priority is member retention tactics (ambassador program, referral incentive).

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Start with your existing data TODAY** — Pull last 12 months of member payments, churn rates, and class attendance. You don't need perfect systems; spreadsheet analysis beats waiting. This gives you your baseline and reveals seasonal patterns (New Year spikes, summer drops).

• **Build a simple 3-scenario model** — Best case (10% member growth), realistic (5%), worst case (decline). For each, calculate: fixed costs (rent, staff), variable costs (supplies per member), and revenue. Run this monthly for the next 12 months. This shows you your break-even point and cash runway.

• **Forecast specifically around your capacity constraint** — How many members can your current space/staff handle profitably? This is THE number that drives everything. Once you know it, you can model when you need to hire or expand, and reverse-engineer the investment required.

• **Link forecasts directly to your pricing/membership decisions** — Don't forecast in a vacuum. Test: "If I raise monthly membership by £5, what's the churn impact?" Model it. If you launch a premium tier or corporate packages, forecast uptake conservatively and see the revenue impact.

• **Schedule a monthly 30-min review** — Actual vs. forecast, adjust assumptions. If real churn is 8% instead of your predicted 5%, your forecast resets immediately. Speed matters more than accuracy here.

**Action this week:** Grab your last 12 months of bank statements and member data. Build that 3-scenario spreadsheet. You'll have clarity by Friday.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**Do this TODAY in a spreadsheet:**

- **Revenue side**: Pull last 3 months of actual sales by service type. For classes, calculate per-slot revenue (total class income ÷ total class hours delivered). For PT, cost per session booked. For add-ons (merchandise, supplements, etc.), actual transaction data. Don't estimate—use real numbers.

- **Cost allocation that matters**: Assign only *direct* costs per service (instructor pay, equipment wear, product COGS). Skip splitting rent/utilities—that's noise right now. You need to see which services subsidize others. PT likely has higher margins than group classes; find out by how much.

- **The insight you're hunting**: Identify which service has the worst margin. That's your first lever—either raise price, reduce instructor cost (fewer smaller classes?), or cut it. If classes run at thin margins but drive memberships, that's *strategic*, not profitable—document that assumption clearly.

- **Weekly update cadence**: Build this so you can update it in 10 minutes every Friday. If margins shift, you'll spot it fast. This isn't a one-time audit—it's your operating dashboard.

**Action this week**: Get 3 months of clean sales data into one sheet. The insight will emerge immediately once you see the numbers side-by-side.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations - Tactical Breakdown

• **Start with your CRM/email platform audit TODAY** — Log into Zapier, Make, or whatever tool runs your automations. Export a list of active workflows. Cross-reference against your booking system (Mindbody, Zen Planner, etc.). You'll spot duplicate triggers immediately — e.g., two "welcome" sequences firing on signup, or multiple SMS reminders for the same trial. Kill the duplicates ruthlessly.

• **Map the actual enquiry→trial journey on paper/Miro** — Walk through as a real prospect: website form submission → email confirmation → booking link sent → SMS reminder → trial booked. Flag every step. Test it yourself using a dummy email address. This reveals broken links, missing steps, or automations that fire but don't connect to your booking system.

• **Pressure-test the weak points** — The enquiry-to-trial flow usually breaks at: (1) email not arriving in spam folders, (2) booking link pointing to wrong trial class/time, (3) SMS going out with no clear CTA. Ask your last 5 trial bookers which step confused them. Fix that specific friction.

• **Set a monthly audit calendar reminder** — Once cleaned up, automations drift. Schedule 30 mins on the 1st of each month to spot new duplicates and test the full flow again with a test signup.

What automation platform are you using? That changes the specific fix slightly.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — 3 Quick Wins

**Pick these 3 tasks (highest ROI):**

• **Membership inquiry responses** — Grace or staff are likely answering the same questions 20+ times/week (pricing, class times, trial sessions). Build a chatbot on WhatsApp or your website using Typeform + Zapier that handles initial qualification, books trial slots directly into your calendar, and flags serious leads for Grace to call. Setup: 2 hours max.

• **Class booking reminders + no-show reduction** — automate SMS reminders 24hrs before classes and again 2hrs before. This directly fills drop-in slots and reduces the "I forgot" cancellations eating your revenue. Use a tool like Calendly Pro or Mindbody's built-in automations. Saves staff 30+ minutes weekly.

• **Member feedback collection post-class** — after every class, auto-send a 2-question SMS (1-click rating + NPS). Captures real retention signals without manual follow-up. Use Typeform or SurveyMonkey webhooks. This data tells you *which classes/coaches* are driving loyalty before members ghost.

**Action this week:**
Start with **#2 (reminders)** — it's fastest to deploy, directly impacts attendance revenue, and requires zero new software spend if you're already using a booking system. Gets you a quick win to build momentum before tackling the chatbot.

Which of these three has been the biggest time-suck for you?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Implementation for The Forge Website

**This is foundational risk management, not growth — but it unblocks revenue growth.** Here's the execution:

• **Audit first, spend second:** Run a free security scan (use Sucuri or Qualys) before hiring anyone. You likely need SSL certificate (essential), basic firewall rules, and stronger password protocols — not a full overhaul. Gets you 80% protection for 20% of typical cost.

• **Make it a vendor decision, not yours:** Contact your website host/provider today and ask them to enable their security package (most include basic DDoS, malware scanning, backups). They handle it; you don't become the expert. Negotiate it into your renewal.

• **Create one password system now:** Move all logins (website, email, payment processor, social) into Bitwarden or 1Password family plan (~£3-5/month). Train staff on it this week. Single biggest vulnerability isn't hackers — it's Grace and team using "password123" across platforms.

• **Link it to member trust:** Once secure, use this in your next marketing message ("Your data is encrypted and protected"). Women-only gyms attract safety-conscious members — cybersecurity is a legitimate selling point.

• **Set a 6-month review, not ongoing panic:** Pick a date to reassess (January 2026). Don't let this become a distraction. Most gyms never get breached because they're low-target. Your time is better spent acquiring members.

**Timeline: 2-3 weeks to implement properly.**

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your CPL has quadrupled (£6 → £27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% (£435 → £109). This isn't budget efficiency—it's underfunding killing your algorithm. Meta needs consistent daily spend to optimise; July's spend is too low to build meaningful learning data or reach saturation. Your close rate (75%) remains stellar, so the product works; the funnel is simply starved.

The lead volume cliff (69 → 4) correlates directly with spend reduction, not creative fatigue or audience exhaustion. April's £6 CPL at scale proves your targeting and creative can work efficiently—you've gone backwards through budget constraint, not performance degradation.

**Actions**

**Immediately restore spend to £400–500/month minimum.** July proved nothing except that underfunding breaks Meta's delivery. You need £13–16/day as a floor to stay in the algorithm's good graces. Allocate this before testing anything else.

**Resurrect April's exact targeting and creative approach.** You don't have a creative problem—you have a budget problem masquerading as one. Pull the ad sets, audiences, and copy from April (69 leads at £6 each). Rebuild that exact campaign at proper spend levels first.

**Test two audience expansions in parallel within that budget:** (1) Lookalike audience built from your April/May customers (highest LTV segment), and (2) Interest-based expansion to "fitness," "women's health," "wellness" at Belfast + 15-mile radius. Keep both small (20–30% of budget each) to measure incrementality without cannibalising core performance.

**Single biggest lever:** Spend £400 next month, not £109. You're not optimising—you're experimenting with poverty. Your close rate and April unit economics prove the model works at scale. The only variable you've changed is budget, and it's tanked results.
